title: The Mathematics of Secrecy
description: Trace the mathematical foundations of cryptography — from modular arithmetic in ancient ciphers to linear algebra and information theory.

stops.label: Caesar Cipher
stops.note: Modular arithmetic (shift by n mod 26) — the simplest mathematical cipher.

stops.label: Polybius Square
stops.note: Coordinate encoding — converting letters to number pairs using a 5×5 grid.

stops.label: Vigenère Cipher
stops.note: Polyalphabetic substitution — repeating key addition over modular arithmetic.

stops.label: Hill Cipher
stops.note: Matrix multiplication mod 26 — the first cipher based on linear algebra.

stops.label: Bifid Cipher
stops.note: Fractionation — splitting coordinates and recombining for diffusion.

stops.label: Trifid Cipher
stops.note: Three-dimensional coordinate fractionation, extending Bifid's mathematical principle.

stops.label: One-Time Pad
stops.note: Shannon's proof of perfect secrecy — information-theoretic security.

stops.label: Modern Cryptography
stops.note: Number theory, elliptic curves, and computational hardness assumptions.
